title: Make and Share
developmental_frame: At 9–10, money is barely abstract and profit means little — but the idea that you can make something real that another person genuinely *wants* is powerful and graspable. A 'mini-business' at this age is very simple: handmade cards, a helper coupon, a small treat. The reward isn't earnings; it's the discovery that making something good and offering it can lead to a real 'yes, please' or 'thank you'. Keep any money tiny and the loop short.
authentic_value_meaning: Value here means a real person *chooses* to have the thing you made — a genuine 'yes' or a delighted 'thank you', not just a grown-up being kind. It's the very first taste of creating something others actually want, which is the seed of every business.

motivation_lever.mechanism: The child makes something small and real, then offers it to a real person and finds out if they truly want it. Even without money, a real person choosing your thing feels different from pretend play — it's a small, safe, genuine stake. Landing that first honest 'yes' is a memorable milestone that makes them want to make the next thing.

artifact: One small thing made and offered to a real person, with the honest response recorded (a genuine yes/thank-you, or a no with its reason).
success_criteria: The child made something real to offer.
They offered it to a real person outside their own household or immediate family.
They can say, in the person's own words, why the answer was yes or no.

lessons.title: What would make someone happy?
lessons.concept: To make something people want, first notice what would make a real person happy. Watch and listen — what does someone near you enjoy, need, or wish for?
lessons.real_example: A girl noticed her neighbour loved her garden but had no plant labels. Little painted labels were a perfect gift — because she spotted what would make *that* person happy.
lessons.apply_task: Think of one real person and write down two things that might make them happy that you could make or do. Pick one.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Did you pick something *they'd* like, or something *you'd* like to make? The best pick is a bit of both.
lessons.feedback_task: If you can, gently ask that person (or someone who knows them) what they enjoy. Adjust your idea.
lessons.teach_back_task: Help a sibling think of something that would make a specific family member happy.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: A computer can't see who's around you or what they'd love. This noticing is yours — it's the real skill.
success_criteria: One idea is chosen based on what a real, specific person would want.

lessons.title: Make one good one
lessons.concept: Make *one* really good version, not lots of rushed ones. A good thing earns a real 'yes'. A sloppy thing, however many you make, earns polite nos.
lessons.real_example: One beautifully painted plant label makes the neighbour say 'oh, I'd love some!' Ten smudged ones don't. Quality does the asking for you.
lessons.apply_task: Make one really good version of your thing. Take your time and make it something you'd be proud to give.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Would you be happy to receive this yourself? If not, what would make it better?
lessons.feedback_task: Show your one good version to someone honest and improve it once based on what they say.
lessons.teach_back_task: Explain to a friend why one good one beats ten rushed ones.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: The making is yours to do well. If it's something on a screen, you should still understand and control how it's made — don't hand the whole job to a computer.
success_criteria: One good version exists, improved once after honest feedback.

lessons.title: Offer it for real
lessons.concept: The big moment: actually offer your thing to a real person and ask if they'd like it. Whether it's a gift or a small trade, a real person really choosing is what makes it count.
lessons.real_example: The girl knocked on the neighbour's door: 'I made these plant labels — would you like some?' The neighbour's happy 'yes please!' was the whole reward.
lessons.apply_task: Offer your thing to a real person (beyond your own home). Ask kindly if they'd like it. Notice their honest reaction.
lessons.reflection_prompt: How did it feel to offer something you made? A little nervous? That nervous-brave feeling is part of it.
lessons.feedback_task: Notice exactly what they said and did. Write it down — those real words are gold.
lessons.teach_back_task: Tell a family member about your offer and how you asked, so they could try it too.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: No device can make the offer for you — the little bit of bravery in asking a real person is exactly what you're practising.
success_criteria: The thing was genuinely offered to a real person and their reaction noted.

lessons.title: What happened?
lessons.concept: Look at what happened. A 'yes' or 'thank you' means you made something wanted — brilliant. A 'no' isn't bad; it usually comes with a reason that helps you make something even better next time.
lessons.real_example: If someone says 'no thanks, I don't have plants', that's not a fail — it tells you to offer plant labels to gardeners next time. The no taught you who to ask.
lessons.apply_task: Write down what happened and why. If it was a yes, what made them happy? If a no, what was their reason?
lessons.reflection_prompt: What's the one thing you learned that would make your next 'make and share' go better?
lessons.feedback_task: Talk it over with a parent: what would you make, or who would you offer to, next time?
lessons.teach_back_task: Tell someone the story of your make-and-share and the one thing it taught you.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Afterward, if you're puzzling over a 'no', you could talk it through with an adult or even AI — but the real lesson came from the real person, and that's what counts most.
success_criteria: The outcome and its reason are recorded in the person's own words.
The child named one thing to do differently next time.
